result:
The old woman at the farmer's market sells honey in mason jars, and every Saturday she tells me the same thing: "The bees know." She never elaborates. I buy a jar anyway, mostly because her hands shake when she counts change and I want her to feel useful. The honey tastes like summer afternoons from a childhood I'm not sure I actually had.

I've been thinking about permanence lately, or the lack of it. Yesterday I found a receipt in my jacket pocket from a bookstore that closed three years ago. The ink had faded to pale blue hieroglyphics. I could barely make out the title of whatever I'd bought, something about learning Spanish in thirty days. Never opened it. The receipt felt more valuable than the book ever was—evidence of an ordinary Tuesday when I still believed I might become someone who speaks multiple languages at dinner parties.

My neighbor grows tomatoes on his fire escape, which is probably illegal, but the building manager pretends not to notice. The plants spill over the railing like they're trying to escape, reaching toward something none of us can see. He gave me one last week, still warm from the sun. I ate it with salt over the sink, and for a moment I understood why people write poetry about simple things. Then my phone buzzed with seventeen work emails and the moment passed, as moments do.

There's a specific type of loneliness that comes with watching the sunrise alone. Not sad, exactly. More like you're witnessing something that requires a witness, and you're not quite enough. I've been waking up at 5 AM without meaning to, sitting by the window with coffee that's always too strong or too weak. The city looks different at that hour—softer, like it's forgotten to put up its defenses.

My grandmother used to say that every person you meet takes a little piece of you with them. I thought she meant it romantically, tragically. Now I think she meant it literally. The barista who remembers my order has a piece. The man who held the subway door last Tuesday has a piece. Even the telemarketer I hung up on probably grabbed a sliver on the way out. No wonder we feel so scattered.

I started writing lists of things I want to remember: The way rain sounds on different surfaces. The specific shade of green that trees turn right before a storm. How my mother pronounces "comfortable" with all four syllables. The lists keep getting longer but my memory keeps getting shorter, like I'm bailing water from a boat with a teaspoon.

Sometimes I walk through expensive furniture stores just to sit on couches I'll never buy. The salespeople hover nervously, but I've perfected an expression that suggests I'm considering a major purchase. Really, I just need to sit somewhere that doesn't know my failures. Those couches have supported no crying jags, no 3 AM anxiety spirals, no pizza-for-dinner-again shame. They're blank slates, those couches. Pure potential energy.

The woman in the apartment above mine practices violin at odd hours. Not well, but with determination that borders on violence. Last night she played the same passage forty-three times (I counted) before something crashed and the music stopped. This morning I saw her carrying the violin case to the dumpster. By noon, she was carrying it back up. We made eye contact but didn't speak. Some struggles are too personal for words.

I've been trying to locate the exact moment when "someday" became "probably never." It must have happened gradually, like gaining weight or losing hair—imperceptible day to day until suddenly your pants don't fit and your scalp shows through. All those somedays piled up in storage somewhere, growing mold.

The honey woman was right, though I'm not sure about what. Maybe bees know that sweetness takes time and collective effort. Maybe they know that individual sacrifice serves something larger. Or maybe they just know to dance when they find something good, to share the coordinates with whoever's listening.

Last week I googled "how to be happy" at 2 AM and felt simultaneously like the saddest and most ridiculous person alive. The internet offered 73 million results in 0.4 seconds. I didn't click any of them. Instead, I ate cereal in bed and watched videos of people restoring old furniture until the sun came up. There's something hopeful about taking something broken and making it functional again, even if it's someone else doing it to someone else's chair.

The truth is, most days feel like rough drafts of better days I might have later. But lately I'm wondering if the rough draft is all we get—if waiting for the final version means missing the crossed-out words and margin notes that might matter more than whatever clean copy we're hoping for.

Tomorrow I'll buy more honey. The old woman will tell me the bees know, and I'll nod like I understand. Maybe understanding isn't the point. Maybe the point is showing up, exchanging currency for sweetness, believing there's wisdom in the gathering of small things. Maybe the bees know that too.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
